title: The 2021 Holberg Conversation with Martha C. Nussbaum
text:
[Music]
professor
martha craven nusbaum
2021
holberg laureate
first of all a heartfelt congratulations
thank you very much and very good to
meet you
likewise absolutely
now in in preparing uh for this uh this
interview this this talk
um
it's it's become ever more clear what a
daunting task it is because i think
um although you are a very public figure
and and everyone knows
uh about this or that side of martha
anderson uh it's just astonishing the
the the number of topics that you cover
the number of subjects and fields that
you that you made make an impact in
and how you manage to combine them and
often break borders or
barriers
so
in one way it it it's such a broad
landscape and so many things uh that
it's uh it's difficult to see how to
sort of
even get started on on on your massive
work in in the course of a conversation
but but you've given us some some help
uh in i think saying
something very crucial about sort of
common denominator and uh an origin of
of the the engagement for if not all
then a lot of your important work and
that is that it's kind of a reflection
and investigation into
human uh um vulnerability
uh
and i think one really good place to to
to start here is with your your theory
of the emotions
in order to get more of a uh um
concretization of what this this might
mean
uh the idea of of um
of this fragility of goodness uh to use
to use a well-known phrase
uh and one thing that's characteristic
of your
of your um uh approach to the emotions
is in one word that they are
cognitive
now to many cognitive would simply mean
well then probably it's it's on the form
of language so it's like a verbal thing
but that's uh obviously not quite what
you have in mind so so specifically when
we talk about the emotions
what what does it mean that they are
cognitive
well
okay the i use cognitive in the way that
most scientists who work on animal
emotions use it namely
cognitive faculties or faculties that
are capable of taking in and processing
information about the world and of
course by now
scientists agree that virtually all
animals have emotions
they have to have them because they
can't find out what's going on in the
world if they weren't endowed with the
wherewithal to have fear
and
love and grief
then they would be
world blind and they probably wouldn't
survive so emotions are a very important
survival mechanism and scientists by now
think that they belong to pretty much
all vertebrates and maybe some
invertebrates
but in human beings
i think the cognitive nature of emotions
is
socially transmitted that's also true
for quite a lot of other animals
we learn
i think we're endowed
at birth with a capacity to have fear
particularly fear that's the earliest
emotion
and then as time goes on society
influences what we fear who we fear
and then we develop other emotions that
we did not have at birth for example
anger requires causal thinking you have
to think someone did something bad to me
so anger is not something that very
young babies have but researchers now
think that children as young as one year
old already have anger they have
experiments where children look at
puppet plays you know where one puppet
hits another with a stick and they
notice that these children before they
can even use language display pleasure
or displeasure at what's going on in
these puppet shows so in that way
they've been able to pinpoint
pre-linguistic anger
there are some that are even later i
would say compassion
is one of the latest ones because
compassion requires perspectival
thinking i have to look at the world
from someone else's point of view in
order to see that that person is
suffering something bad so that can't
develop right away but i think pretty
soon we are
mind readers so to speak we are able to
inhabit the perspective of someone else
and although there i think only some
animals have compassion only the ones
that are capable of inhabiting the
perspective of some other creature
pretty much all humans have that
and so we
develop those abilities as time goes on
now there the problem kicks in because
society teaches us
a lot about what things are valuable
what things are not valuable and in that
way shapes our emotions
so the emotions that we have are not
necessarily very reliable
we have fear
of course about some things that are
really fearful
death and illness and so forth but we
learn to fear a lot of things that are
not really bad
like people from other racial groups
we're often taught to fear people who
look different from us
some people are taught to fear people
from other religions so society gets to
work napping the things we fear the
things for which we have grief
and
sometimes they that's rightly done and
it's normatively good but very often
there's distortion so when you think
about compassion
i think pretty much every human being
has compassion
for these sufferings of some other
people
but which ones
most people feel compassion only for
people who are like them
we all feel compassion when our family
members are ill
but of course somebody on the other side
of the world
and let's say now the people i work a
lot on india so i'll mention india
people in india who are suffering from
the terrible covid situation
most americans don't know much about
that
and most americans do not feel a strong
emotion of compassion or if they do
because they just read the newspaper
it doesn't last very long it goes away
very quickly
so our strongest and most motivating
emotions unfortunately are very narrow
and very one-sided and i think one of
the great tasks of moral development
is to broaden our emotions so that we
have compassion for people we don't know
people in other parts of the world and
so on
but it's really very very difficult you
know and i've been corresponding a lot
with people who say oh now it's so great
that kovid has gone away
what they mean is that here in chicago
or maybe other parts of the u.s
things are much better but they're not
even thinking about other countries in
which it's getting worse so that's the
trouble with the way people are and we
really have to work against that but all
of that is cognitive we take in views
both good and bad about what's important
what's not important and then our
emotions follow along with that to
follow up on this it it's uh
on the one hand uh the the
uncovering of the emotions as
um
as cognitive then means that there is
perhaps hope of altering them as well of
uh for instance like carrying out a sort
of critique of the emotions uh coming to
see their their shortcomings and also
coming to see that it's not as if uh if
it's an emotion then it's good it's if
it's an emotion we have to ask which
emotion and which shape does it have in
which history well and it's not just
which emotion but it's which
object of the particular
no one would probably say that all fear
is bad we got to have some fear or else
we'd all be dead if we didn't have fear
we would walk out into traffic we
wouldn't protect ourselves against
disease but it's a question of what what
objects or
the fears directed toward and how
vulnerable it is to rhetorical
manipulation
so yeah there's
it's both good news and bad news right
if the emotions were just hardwired
then we wouldn't be able to change them
but at least they'd be pretty stable
and they couldn't be manipulated by
political dictators
but because they can be socially
manipulated
they
can get worse
but of course they can also get better
if you compare human emotions to the
emotions of other animals i think you
see this right away because if i think
of the emotions of an elephant
elephants
feel fear
they feel love they feel grief
but it's all very steady and stable
because most of their emotions are based
on
both inherited tendencies and then a
kind of social learning in the group
that's pretty
stable and pretty constant over time
human emotions are all over the place
one day you might feel
good about something and then a
political
speech is made you're very swayed by
that and you flip over to the other side
and and so we're very unstable in our
emotions so yeah you're you're right
that the fact that they're uh cognitive
not only cognitive but socially
shaped that's good news in a way but
it's also bad news because it means they
can so easily be misshapen and distorted
so i think that the job that we have to
do is to really have a good normative
theory
and try to
get societies and families
to shape children's emotions in the
right way
now in a way we know that
parents always know that they want their
children
to sh to control their anger
not to feel anger at some other child
who's playing with them and
so they they shape their children's
emotions and they say well now look that
was not something you should feel angry
about or they also encourage compassion
look at what happened to susan she she
lost
her toy that she was playing with
you should really help her you should
feel feel sorry for her so parents shape
children's emotions in many ways
but of course sometimes they do it very
badly they encourage racism they
encourage sexism
and almost without knowing it there are
a lot of experiments where people are
given a baby to hold and the baby is not
labeled as to whether it's male or
female
and right away the experimental subjects
impute emotions to that baby
because of the sex that it's labeled as
so for example they hand the baby to me
i'm the experimental subject and the
experimenter says oh please hold her for
me
now then the experimental subject
typically says oh poor thing she's so
frightened and they hold the baby close
but if the experimenter says please hold
him for me
they behave very differently they bounce
the baby in the air and they say oh he's
angry he wants to get what he wants so
they're shaping
the emotions of that baby by the very
way they hold the baby by the very way
they name the emotions because after all
the baby is just crying and one cry is
pretty much like another
and as time goes on
the idea that little girls are fearful
and timid and that boys are assertive
and aggressive takes root in the child
and in the family so that's the kind of
thing that happens to distort emotions
and
so even even it may be
that even our fear of death is a
distortion there all right my my own
work on this began with the ancient
greeks and romans and the ancient greeks
and romans were very extreme about this
they said that pretty much all the
emotions that we learn in society
are based on false beliefs
namely they're based on the belief that
something outside yourself that's not in
the control of your own reason and will
is very very important
now they thought that nothing that's not
in the control of your rational will
is very important
you should just
retreat into your inner world so to
speak
and say well all of that stuff outside
that's nothing to me
but of course most people are not like
that
they think that the deaths of loved ones
are terrible
they think that their own illnesses are
terrible so seneca and the stoics
and indeed the epicureans too said we
should not have
those fears
and the beliefs that underlie them
are false beliefs namely that illness is
something terribly important etc
so then they set about to try to teach
people to root up the emotions they
wanted to eradicate them totally and
have people who were
stable
content with their own reasoning
and not affected by the outside things
in the world
now i don't believe that the normative
view they have is correct i think that
they were right about some things i
think it's probably bad to be too upset
when you lose some money
it's bad to be upset at insults to your
honor and reputation they talk about
that kind of thing a lot
but you know
if your loved one dies
of course it's right to be terribly
upset so i think so my view is a lot
more complicated and it means that
basically what happens in greek tragedy
gives us a good road map to follow as we
educate our emotions
my work on emotions began with work on
greek tragedy and i think there's good
reason for that although i didn't really
know it at the time
namely greek tragedies show
bad things happening
but they are the things that really are
important
the loss of a country the loss of a city
the deaths of loved ones
yourself being put into slavery
all of these things
are really really important and
therefore we should
feel
fear of that happening we should feel
grief about those things happening and
when it happens to somebody else we
should feel a powerful compassion so i
think the greek tragedies are for the
most part
a road map of the important emotions but
notice that they do not show people
being very very upset when they lose a
little money
that's more like the stuff of comedy
people who behave like that are laughed
at and they're
silly and so we kind of get an
immoral education out of tragedy some
things are really worth caring about and
some things are not and i actually think
that tragedy which was of course
performed for the whole city adults and
young people alike
it really was a form of moral education
for the emotions of the citizens we
don't have anything like that now
because there's no really the arts can't
be public in the same way there's no way
of having an entertainment that everyone
comes to and you sit
on a holiday and you see all your fellow
citizens around you
but we can turn to the art still for
examples of emotions
and i think we still do have some
public emotion festivals so to speak
particularly
in the visual arts like a work of
architecture
can educate the emotions of a city
i've talked about millennium park in
downtown chicago which is a park
that celebrates civic unity and it
celebrates it both with serious images
of
joy and
celebration
and with comic images because there's a
thing called the crown fountain that
displays different chicagoans of
different races and ages but their
images are distorted on a large screen
and then water shoots out of the mouth
of the image so it's really very funny
if you stand there and watch it and i
think it's very inviting people always
want to go in the pool and paddle around
and it it brings us together in laughter
at the human body and how strange it is
so there are different ways that cities
and countries can have of bringing
people together in emotion celebrations
we've just had yesterday or monday
rather our memorial memorial day where
we have a communal celebration in honor
of soldiers who have died in different
wars and that's another way the
countries celebrate
important things coming together around
central losses that we all share as a
nation
so i think despite the fact that modern
nations are too big to have greek tragic
festivals there's still things we can do
and that's why i think the arts are such
an incredibly important part of our
public culture both
music and literature theater
so one of the most successful dramas of
recent years
in the u.s but i think it's been in
europe a lot too
is lin-manuel miranda's play hamilton
it's of course about the founding of the
united states and it contains many
emotions the characters some of them are
aspiring with energy and joy to create
something new
and then there's aaron burr who feels
envy and he wants to harm hamilton
because hamilton is getting good things
that he's not getting
and the audience is enlisted on the side
of creation
and joy
and we learn to disapprove of envy and
spite that tries to harm somebody who's
doing something good
and of course all of this is set in the
context of the american founding but
it's performed by a group of
multi-racial actors so what we learn to
understand is that our founding
was not just by white people it was by
people of various races hamilton himself
was biracial but we also learned that we
have to be
in it together where we don't do
anything fine at all so that's an
example of a work that's
educates the citizens so to speak and i
think does it really well
but of course you have to pay a lot of
money to get a ticket and it's you know
the typical thing with broadway stage
it's very exclusionary in terms of who
actually gets to see it live so so the
arts need to think much more about how
we
bring people in
bring people together without
segmenting them into the people who have
a lot of money and the people who don't
but anyway that's a way that cultures
can
educate the emotions of citizens
of course the greeks when they
criticized the emotions they they said
something that's quite right namely that
they all have something in common they
all impute a very high value to things
outside yourself that you don't fully
control
now there may be exceptions i think
perhaps the emotions of wonder and awe
may be exceptions to that but most of
the major emotions
if you once form a strong attachment to
something outside yourself let's take
the example of a child then you'll have
fear when that child is threatened
you'll have anger when somebody else
damages the child
and of course you have ongoing love for
the child
you'll have compassion when the child is
suffering
and then pretty much all the others
follow along with that so yeah they they
have this one thing in common and so the
stoics thought
that if you can only learn to get rid of
these unwise attachments to things
outside yourself
then you get rid of them with one stroke
now they also knew that that was very
hard to do so in their practical
teaching they tried to work on
specific emotions and they conducted
therapeutic treatments of anger that
were different from their therapeutic
treatments of grief
but in the end of the day it all comes
down to
what you love
if you don't love anything but your own
rational will
then you don't get bothered by any of
these emotions at all
yes exactly so so
what do you show i think beautifully for
instance in in the therapy of desire is
the the drama of this how
how one might wish to hold on to
some emotions because they seem like
good and excellent things and then other
things do not seem good so one might
then dream of of we want to hold on to
love because love is beautiful and great
but we don't want fear or grief or anger
but but as you show so beautifully in
that book too
um
you don't
it's not easy to get one without the
other so if you have love for someone
outside yourself uh and there's a threat
to them you you feel fear and if someone
hurts them you feel anger if if it
should come to the worst you feel grief
so it's it seems as if in that classical
critique and your presentation of it
it's very much a sort of take it or
leave it packaged almost it is in in a
way but i've
made
refinements on that as the years have
gone on
first of all i think that the emotion of
disgust which is very damaging socially
and politically
is one that we can
diminish greatly so okay disgust is a
very strong reaction
but it's still cognitive and what
researchers who work on it show is that
it always involves ideas of bodily
you don't want to be touched or you
don't want to ingest something that you
view as polluting now the things that
our objects have discussed
are animal parts
and animals that remind you of your own
animality so they call it animal
reminders
and why do we have disgusted animal
reminders well because we don't want to
see ourselves as animal and mortal
ourselves but of course that's a lie
so even the basic experience of disgust
toward
feces bodily fluids and slimy bugs and
so on even that is based on
self-deception
you're trying to convince yourself
you're above all that
even though that probably doesn't do
very much harm it's worth noting that
it's still based on a kind of falsehood
you're telling to yourself oh that's not
part of me let me get rid of that i'm
not really an animal
but then what's worse
is that in all known societies and we've
done some cross-cultural research on
that with our center in new delhi
learn to feel disgust at other groups of
people who in that society
stand in for animals so to speak because
it's not enough to keep yourself clean
away from feces and bodily dirt and so
on but people need a
further buffer zone so to speak and then
they fasten on a group of humans and
they can say about these humans oh those
are the animals and if i separate myself
from those then i'm that much further
from being animal and mortal myself
now
in every society there are some such
groups
they're always different and the shape
of the discussed fantasy is different
so that was why the cross-cultural work
with india was so interesting the indian
cast hierarchy teaches people that
members of the lowest castes
who typically are selected for that
because they
dispose of wastes and they sweep the
floor and so on
those are the animals and you must not
touch them you must not allow them into
your kitchen any food that's been
touched by them is polluted and so on
now
we compared that with racial disgust
directed at african americans in the
history of the u.s
and it's fascinating to see that some of
the themes are constant namely oh those
are
based animals we shouldn't really touch
them we shouldn't let them swim in the
same swimming pool we shouldn't let them
drink from the same water supply
but there's some big differences because
for example in the old south an
african-american woman often did cook
food for the whole family
the
family had no objection to that whereas
in india
that would be the worst thing for a
dollar to cook food in your kitchen
so all these things are so
irrational that you once you put them
side by side you see the complete
irrationality of it but of course they
focus on not sharing water
not sharing sex of course
and in general just not having enough
contact with those animals
now those are two examples of that but
they're others i think in most cultures
women are treated as more animal than
the male because they're connected with
giving birth and so they're thought to
be as it were the body of the male the
male can be sort of like an angel with
no body but the woman because she gives
birth she's the body
and so people learn to feel disgust at
women's
menstrual periods at their birth
experiences
and at other aspects of women's bodies
in my book the monarchy of fear i have a
whole list of statements about women
made by donald trump that illustrate
this this fact he he has a particular
preoccupation with discuss at women's
bodies so if a woman was having a
menstrual period if she was about to get
up and go to the bathroom
he would make some remark oh that's so
disgusting so anyway these forms of
disgust
are extremely
dangerous and damaging for society
and they're not necessary they're all
learned i mean if somebody didn't grow
up in that culture they think this is a
very bizarre formation
but
the question is can we unlearn them
enough
now we probably can't get rid of the
first what i call primary object
discussed discussed at bodily waste
products
because it's probably
ingrained in our evolutionary endowment
and maybe it's even useful sometimes
because it steers us away from things
that are really dangerous like if the
milk smells disgusting you probably had
better not drink it it's a good guide to
things that are really dangerous
but as i say even that kind of disgust
is dangerous because it spreads the view
that animality is itself disgusting
so
all of that
could stay but we would have to be on
guard
but what i call projective disgust when
you project it onto a group of people
that's always bad there should be no
such thing in any society
can it happen
could we ever find a society that had no
projective disgust
well not yet
it migrates if it's not
a racial subgroup it might be sexual
subgroup feeling discussed at
transgender people for example is a big
thing in the united states today
but we can work on that and i think we
could
it's certainly thinkable that we could
lose completely projective discussed
another thing that i think is thinkable
that we could lose without losing the
others
is the retributive kind of anger
now anger is very complicated it
contains more than one thought
it contains the thought
that
thing that that damage that happened
was wrong and it should not have
happened
but it also contains and this is what
every philosopher who defines anger in
both india and the united states has
said it contains the thought there
should be a payback the person who did
that should pay for what he or she has
done so the thought of retribution comes
in
now i think
that when you're angry
the retributive part is actually very
damaging
and very useless
it's just makes you turn to the past not
to the future
it makes you focus on destroying the
person who did that to you rather than
having a better world in the future if
you think about capital punishment for
example
very popular in the united states and
it's popular despite the fact that we
know from countless experiments that it
does no good
it does not stop people from killing
but people
cling to it
if their child has been murdered
they think oh now i better make sure
that that killer gets killed
and sometimes people get very obsessed
with that and they really devote their
whole lives to making sure that that
killer gets capital punishment
but what good does that do
it doesn't do any good and what they
really want is that their child should
come back and of course that's not
possible but what might be possible
would be for them to do something to
help other children
or to prevent other murders in the
future the group called mothers against
drunk driving has done a lot to stop
people from driving when they're drunk
so even though those people don't get
their children back
there are a lot of other children that
don't get killed so that's constructive
and it's future directed
but the kind that turns to the past and
says let's get rid of that
killer that's no use at all i think
again about getting a divorce when you
get a divorce it's very very tempting to
think that what that process should all
be about is
destroying the person who hurt you
you want that person to suffer
how do you make them suffer well often
it's by trying to get a punitive
financial settlement in the divorce
court
but really that doesn't do any good for
you
it really doesn't help you move forward
it doesn't help your self-respect it
doesn't help you become happy again
and then you get focused on the past and
you devote all your thinking to that
person who hurt you in the past
and it often does great damage to your
family to your children
so again that retributive type of anger
that retributive part of anger is
actually very destructive
so what i think is we could get rid of
that part
and still keep the other part that says
this is wrong and it should not happen
again
i give this the name transition anger
because it turns it makes a transition
to face the future it says well that was
bad but let's make sure that that
doesn't happen again in the future
martin luther king jr had the same idea
he actually made the the same
distinction that i make and he says that
there's one way you could get angry at
let's say racial discrimination
and that way would be just to try to
inflict pain on the people who caused it
and king said
that's not very helpful
and it's confused it's not even radical
because for it to be radical it has to
do something that's useful
and so he said when people came to his
movement
their anger had to be as he put it
purified meaning it had to lose that
retributive part
and what it would keep
is the spirit of justified protest
and the determination to make a world
that's better in the future but then of
course the
person who says that's wrong it had
better not happen again had better also
have some other emotions such as hope
that it can be made better and faith
that people will join in making it
better and so on so that's what king was
trying to build in his movement i think
that's possible
it's something that people have said in
many times and places buddhist thinkers
in all eras have been against the
retributive kind of anger
gandhi also although he wasn't a
buddhist he agreed with the buddhists in
thinking retributive anger is very
damaging so we can get rid of the
retributive part
and still retain as gandhi of course
always did
the spirit of determined protest against
wrongdoing
and when gandhi
led people on a march he didn't say
let's go and destroy these british
people
no
he says he gave them images of hope
and building
he had them sing songs by the indian
poet ravindranath dagor there were songs
about
how you must keep walking
walking on walking alone until you're
able to create something better so i do
think that that's one place where the
greeks and romans went a little bit
wrong because i do think we can get rid
of the retributive kind of anger and
keep
the good aspect of anger which is
recognizing wrongdoing saying that's not
tolerable we don't want to have that in
the future and then combining the part
of anger that's left the purified part
with other emotions such as hope
and faith to build a better future
so both when it comes to um retributive
the retributive part of anger and
also very fascinatingly uh in your
description of projective disgust
um uh you think that that
change is possible but then there's the
question of what form
should or must the therapy take because
at one extreme for instance you have
someone like me reading
your analysis of projective disgust and
being fascinated and perhaps starting to
wonder about
my reactions of anger and disgust and so
on uh but how far does that get one so
what what would be the forms
of therapy in in this case how do you
envisage this
well i think it has to happen at
all levels of society of course one part
that's very important to me is law
if law gives same-sex couples the right
to marry
then immediately they're positioned
alongside straight couples
they're not positioned in a lower level
of animality as they were in previous
eras of law
so law is a very important part of it
but i also think that informal social
movements are extremely important if you
look at how the gay rights movement has
actually proceeded
they they try to stimulate images of
love
and inclusion
just now we're beginning june is gay
pride month and for the very first time
the whole city of chicago will have the
rainbow flag flown
